angle: Model-driver strategy for /memory:search: whether to default to a driver model (DeepSeek) or make the data-quality verdict render deterministically in the tool layer so driver choice stops mattering. After reading the verdict computation (search-results.ts), the calibration gate (confidence-scoring.ts), the score resolver (pipeline/types.ts), the command + presentation contract, and the benchmark metrics, the finding is: the verdict is ALREADY computed deterministically in the tool layer; the only thing the driver controls is whether it RELAYS the fields. The contract makes the fields optional ("Absence is valid"), which is the sole source of the model-fidelity variance the benchmark measured. So the right move is to make rendering mandatory (not to pin a driver), and separately fix the score-calibration soft spot that feeds the gate the spurious kubernetes=0.78 signal.

title: Make requestQuality/citationPolicy MANDATORY in the render contract instead of pinning a driver model
problem: The benchmark's recommendation is to default /memory:search to DeepSeek (implementation-summary.md:69 'Use DeepSeek for /memory:search dispatches'). But pinning a driver is the wrong lever: the verdict is ALREADY computed deterministically in the tool layer — assessRequestQuality (search-results.ts:433-485) and deriveCitationPolicy (search-results.ts:368-371) derive requestQuality and citationPolicy from scores with zero model involvement; the driver only RELAYS them. The model-fidelity variance the benchmark measured (avgFidelity ranges 5.6 gpt55 to 6.9 deepseek out of 7, metrics.json perModel) is a RENDERING problem, not a computation problem, and its root is that the contract explicitly permits omission: search_presentation.txt:116 'Absence is valid' and search.md:76 'Their absence is valid'. Even the best driver (DeepSeek 6.9/7) drops a field sometimes precisely because the contract sanctions it. Defaulting to DeepSeek masks the variance for one model on one bench; it does not stop any driver from dropping the field, and it adds a model dependency to a command whose verdict is model-independent by construction.
evidence: search-results.ts:368-371 (deriveCitationPolicy), 433-485 (assessRequestQuality), 1161+1176 (citationPolicy set unconditionally on every non-empty search); search_presentation.txt:102-116 ('Absence is valid'); search.md:76,78; metrics.json perModel avgFidelity {deepseek:6.9, mimo:6.7, kimi:6.5, gpt55:5.6}, citeCorrectRate all 1.
proposal: Flip the contract from 'optional when present' to 'mandatory whenever the tool-layer envelope carries them'. In search_presentation.txt section 'Named optional fields' (lines 102-116) and search.md:76, change the rule to: when data.citationPolicy is present in the envelope (it always is on the memory_search path — search-results.ts:1176 sets it unconditionally), the renderer MUST emit the citationPolicy line, and MUST emit requestQuality when data.requestQuality is present. Add this to the §3 self-check (search.md:78) and the section-2 core-slot-style mandate so a dropped verdict fails the pre-emit self-check the same way a dropped score does. Keep the fields as trailing named lines (placement already specified at search_presentation.txt:104-114). This is a contract/spec-doc change only — no TS change — and it makes driver choice irrelevant for the data-quality surface, which is the stated goal.
benefit: Envelope fidelity becomes 7/7 for every driver because the field is no longer droppable by contract. Removes the need to pin a driver model for data-quality reasons, so the command stays model-agnostic and the benchmark's DeepSeek recommendation is reduced to a cost/latency preference (70s vs 271s Kimi) rather than a correctness requirement. citeCorrectRate is already 1.0 for all four models (metrics.json), so mandatory rendering closes the only remaining cross-model gap.
risk: A model that genuinely cannot follow the stricter contract would now visibly fail the self-check rather than silently omit — but that is the intended signal, and all four benchmarked models already render the field most of the time. Low risk: no runtime/TS change, fully reversible by reverting the contract wording.

title: Add an off-corpus relevance floor to the good-verdict gate to kill the kubernetes=0.78 false-good
problem: assessRequestQuality reaches 'good' off raw absolute relevance alone: topScore >= TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD (0.8) returns good outright (search-results.ts via confidence-scoring.ts:471-473), and topScore >= HIGH_THRESHOLD (0.7) with a quality-ratio or margin returns good (confidence-scoring.ts:476-478). The 'absolute relevance' it reads is RAW cosine similarity from sqlite-vec normalized to 0-1 (resolveAbsoluteRelevance, pipeline/types.ts:90-97). Cosine similarity is not zero-anchored: a semantically unrelated off-corpus term still lands near 0.7-0.8 against a generic doc. The benchmark confirms this exactly — perQuery.kubernetes meanTopScore 0.79, scoreSpread 0.05, all four models 'good', queryClass 'off-corpus' (metrics.json), where the top hit is the unrelated Spec Memory Runtime Retention Cleanup summary (implementation-summary.md:67). The gate waved a confident citation through on a spurious match. 'authentication' correctly stays weak at 0.50 only because it happened to score lower; the gate has no off-corpus discrimination, just a fixed cosine threshold. This is NOT solved by sibling phase 027-retrieval-floor-experiment, which measures result-set TAIL size against a min-3 floor and explicitly does NOT change the quality gate (027 spec.md answered_questions: 'it does NOT, it measures and reports a verdict on the tail').
evidence: confidence-scoring.ts:39-40 (HIGH/LOW thresholds 0.7/0.4), 78 (TOP_DOMINANT_THRESHOLD 0.8), 466-478 (good-verdict branches, topMargin computed but unused in dominant branch); pipeline/types.ts:90-97 (resolveAbsoluteRelevance returns raw cosine); metrics.json perQuery.kubernetes {meanTopScore:0.79, scoreSpread:0.05, all good, queryClass off-corpus} vs authentication {0.50, weak}; 027 spec.md answered_questions (floor experiment does not touch the gate).
proposal: Add a relevance-margin / separation test to the good-verdict path in assessRequestQuality (confidence-scoring.ts:471-478). A genuine on-corpus hit has its top score well-separated from the corpus baseline (the mean/median of the lower results), while a spurious off-corpus hit sits in a flat band near the threshold (kubernetes scoreSpread 0.05). Gate 'good' on BOTH topScore >= threshold AND a minimum separation between topScore and the median tail score (or a minimum topMargin to result[1] on the absolute scale — topMargin is already computed at confidence-scoring.ts:466-468 but currently only used in the 0.7-band branch, not the 0.8-dominant branch). Tune the separation threshold against a standing off-corpus query set. Land it behind an env flag (the module already uses search-flags.ts gating for absolute-relevance calibration) so it can be measured before default-on.
benefit: Off-corpus terms that score high on raw cosine but have no real corpus separation drop from 'good' to 'weak', so citationPolicy flips to do_not_cite_results and the gate stops authorizing citations on spurious matches. Fixes the one finding the benchmark says touches 005 data quality directly (implementation-summary.md:67). Because the verdict is deterministic in the tool layer, the fix applies uniformly to every driver model at once.
risk: Over-tightening could push borderline-but-genuine single-strong-hit queries to 'weak', reducing citations on legitimately sparse-but-correct results. Mitigate by measuring against both the off-corpus set (kubernetes/authentication) and a positive on-corpus set before flipping the flag default; the topMargin signal is already wired so the math is incremental, not new.

title: Emit requestQuality/citationPolicy on the memory_context path so the command's DEFAULT route always carries the verdict
problem: The command's preferred retrieval route is memory_context (search.md:46 'Prefer memory_context(...)'), not memory_search. The deterministic verdict (citationPolicy/requestQuality) is built only inside formatSearchResults on the memory_search path (search-results.ts:1161,1173-1176); grep for citationPolicy/requestQuality/assessRequestQuality in handlers/memory-context.ts returns ZERO hits. memory_context wraps an inner memory_search envelope and re-reads innerEnvelope.data.results (memory-context.ts:753,864,883), so when the inner envelope already carried the fields they survive at data.* — but memory_context's own truncation/compaction path rebuilds innerEnvelope.data with an explicit allowlist of {results,count} (memory-context.ts:820-824,883-884) and its impossible-budget fallback synthesizes data:{count:0,results:[]} (memory-context.ts:779), neither of which re-asserts citationPolicy. So on the exact default route, under token pressure, the verdict can be silently dropped before the model ever sees it — independent of which driver is used. This is a tool-layer gap that no driver choice can fix.
evidence: search.md:46 (prefer memory_context); search-results.ts:1161,1173-1176 (fields built only here); zero grep hits for citationPolicy/requestQuality in handlers/memory-context.ts; memory-context.ts:779 (fallback synthesizes bare data), 820-824 & 883-884 (data rebuilt as results+count only), 753/864/883 (reads inner results).
proposal: Guarantee the verdict survives the default route. Two options, preferring (a): (a) in memory-context.ts, when reconstructing innerEnvelope.data during truncation/compaction (lines ~820-824, ~883-884) and in the impossible-budget fallback (~779), carry forward the inner envelope's citationPolicy and requestQuality alongside results/count so they are never dropped by budget pressure; or (b) recompute deriveCitationPolicy/assessRequestQuality at the memory_context boundary from the surviving results. Option (a) is a pure pass-through preserve and matches the existing 'preserve caller metadata' intent already present for memory_search (search-results.ts:1180-1192).
benefit: The data-quality verdict is present on the command's default route in all cases, including under token truncation, so the cite-iff-good guarantee the benchmark validated on memory_search actually holds on the route the command tells the model to prefer. Removes a silent-drop failure mode that is invisible to the model-driver benchmark (which dispatched bare queries that mostly fit budget).
risk: Adds two small fields to context payloads near the token budget — negligible token cost, but must be re-asserted AFTER truncation math so it is not itself trimmed. Confirm the fields land outside the per-result truncation loop. Reversible.

title: Demote the benchmark's 'default to DeepSeek' recommendation to a documented cost/latency note, not a correctness mandate
problem: implementation-summary.md:69 frames the recommendation operationally: 'Use DeepSeek for /memory:search dispatches, MiMo when cost matters... Avoid Kimi... do not run gpt-5.5 below high.' Read literally this invites pinning a driver model into the command's operating guidance. But the same doc's own data shows the data-quality signal is model-independent: 'The data-quality signal the command exposes does not depend on which model drives it' (implementation-summary.md:59) and citeCorrectRate is 1.0 for all four models (metrics.json perModel). The only real cross-model differences are latency (deepseek 70s vs kimi 271s) and envelope fidelity — and fidelity is fixed structurally by proposal 1 (mandatory rendering). The gpt-5.5 'weakest' result is also confounded: it ran at medium against a high trio (implementation-summary.md:86,107 Known Limitations: 'the variant axis is not apples-to-apples'). Treating 'default to DeepSeek' as a correctness rule would add an unnecessary model dependency and bake in a confounded comparison.
evidence: implementation-summary.md:59 (model-independent signal), 69 (recommendation wording), 86 & 107 (gpt-5.5 medium-vs-high confound, Known Limitations); metrics.json perModel citeCorrectRate all 1, avgLatencyMs {deepseek:69975, mimo:89599, gpt55:102751, kimi:270690}, avgTools {deepseek:5.1, mimo:5.1, gpt55:9.8, kimi:14.7}.
proposal: Once proposals 1-3 land, record the driver guidance as a COST/LATENCY preference only: 'For interactive /memory:search dispatch, prefer a fast low-variance driver (DeepSeek ~70s, MiMo ~90s); Kimi over-explores (14.7 tools, 271s) for no verdict gain. Driver choice does not affect the data-quality verdict, which renders deterministically in the tool layer.' Keep it out of the §6 HARD RULES of search.md (which govern correctness) and put it in operator notes. Add the apples-to-apples caveat (gpt-5.5 ran at medium) wherever the 'weakest driver' line is cited downstream so it is not over-read.
benefit: Prevents a confounded benchmark result from hardening into a command-level model mandate. Keeps /memory:search model-agnostic for correctness while still giving operators an honest latency/cost steer. Aligns the recommendation with the doc's own model-independence finding.
risk: Almost none — this is a framing/doc-placement correction. The only risk is operators ignoring the latency steer and using a slow driver (Kimi), which costs wall-clock time but not correctness.
